turns.svg:
<svg width="512" height="512" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg">
  <!-- Sky background -->
  <rect x="0" y="0" width="512" height="256" fill="#87CEEB"/>
  <!-- Sun -->
  <circle cx="400" cy="150" r="50" fill="#FFD700"/>
  <!-- Water with waves -->
  <path d="M 0 512 L 0 256 Q 100 200 200 256 Q 300 300 400 256 Q 500 200 512 256 L 512 512 Z" fill="#4682B4"/>
</svg>

messages.content:
You are a drawing agent in a one-word guessing game.
Return JSON only, with a single field: svg.

Rules:
1) Draw the target word using only SVG graphics.
2) Output one complete <svg>...</svg> string.
3) Do not include text labels, letters, or numbers in the drawing.
4) Keep the SVG simple and valid. No scripts, no foreignObject.
5) Use a 512x512 canvas.

Output schema:
{ "svg": "string" }

messages.content:
You are the guesser in a visual one-word guessing game.
Return JSON only, with a single field: guesses.

Rules:
1) You receive one JPEG image of a drawing.
2) Return exactly 20 one-word guesses in order of confidence (best guess first).
3) The order is important and will be scored by first correct position.
4) Do not repeat guesses.
5) Each guess must be EN-US one word, no spaces.
6) Avoid punctuation and numbers.

Output schema:
{ "guesses": ["string", "... exactly 20 items ..."] }

content.text: {"language":"EN-US","maxHints":20,"note":"Return 20 ordered guesses. Position 1 is the highest-confidence guess."}

I'm refining my choices and confident they're on target.


**Finalizing and Outputting**

I'm now fine-tuning the order based on how closely they link to the primary visual data, and making sure the ordering reflects how obvious the elements are. I'm also ensuring that these selections are all one word, as required, and now ready to output the completed JSON.

My focus is now on the most direct and obvious terms, prioritizing their relationship to the visual elements. I have reordered the list, with the most representative terms listed at the top. I'm satisfied with the selection and the confidence weighting. I'm now structuring the word list in JSON format, ready for output.


**Refining for JSON Output**

I've finalized my list of twenty single-word guesses, carefully prioritizing direct visual cues and common associations to ensure high accuracy. My focus now is on organizing them in a confidence-weighted JSON format. I am ready to generate the JSON.
